notes: Arturia Vocoder V is a 16-band vocoder inspired by classic hardware vocoders including the EMS Vocoder 5000, Sennheiser VSM-201, and Moog 16-band Vocoder. A vocoder works by analysing the spectral envelope of a modulator signal (typically a voice or microphone input) and imposing that envelope onto a carrier signal (the internal synthesizer). The result is the classic robotic, talking-synthesizer effect used in Daft Punk, Kraftwerk, Laurie Anderson, Roger Troutman, and countless others. Carrier cutoff/formant and formant shift are the most expressive real-time controls, while band spread and sibilance refine intelligibility and presence. For best results, use a live microphone or pre-recorded vocal as the modulator input.

Expression.description: Carrier level — scales synthesizer carrier signal level relative to modulator

Modulation.description: Modulation wheel — assignable modulation source for carrier synth

LFO Rate.description: Carrier LFO rate — adds wobble or vibrato to the carrier pitch

Carrier Cutoff.description: Carrier synthesizer filter cutoff — shapes the frequency range of the carrier before vocoding

Formant Shift.description: Formant shift — shifts all analysis bands up or down in frequency, changing perceived vocal register (up = child-like, down = deep)

Band Spread.description: Band spread — increases or decreases the frequency spacing between vocoder analysis bands; wider spread improves intelligibility

Sibilance.description: Sibilance level — controls the level of unvoiced high-frequency content (s, t, f sounds) passed directly to output for intelligibility

Attack.description: Band envelope attack — how quickly each band responds to the modulator; faster = more detailed articulation

Release.description: Band envelope release — how quickly bands close after modulator signal drops; longer = smoother, more sustained vocoder effect

Reverb.description: Reverb send level — adds space and dimension to the vocoder output

Carrier Waveform.description: Carrier oscillator waveform — sawtooth for pitched vocal effect; noise for whispered/unpitched effect
